_meta.description: Quebec Pension Plan (QPP) contributions for self-employed workers. The single biggest financial surprise for first-time self-employed people in Quebec — they pay both the employee and employer share.

title_fr: RRQ (Régime de rentes du Québec) — Cotisations des travailleurs autonomes
plain_language_summary: The QPP is Quebec's equivalent of CPP — a mandatory pension contribution that funds your retirement. When you're employed, your employer pays half and you pay half. When you're self-employed, you pay BOTH halves. This is often the biggest financial shock for new self-employed workers: the QPP alone can cost $5,000–$8,000/year at moderate income levels.

the_big_surprise.title: Why this shocks first-time self-employed workers
the_big_surprise.explanation: An employee earning $60,000 pays $3,600 in QPP (6%). Their employer pays another $3,600. As a self-employed person earning $60,000, YOU pay the full $7,200 (12.8% — both sides). This is in addition to income tax. Many people don't budget for this and face a massive surprise tax bill.
the_big_surprise.action: Budget 12.8% of your expected net income for QPP from day one.

rates_2026.note: Verify exact rates for 2026 at Revenu Québec — rates are indexed annually

calculation.cap: Maximum pensionable earnings of $71,300 — contributions on income above this are not required

how_and_when_to_pay.method: Calculated on your Quebec income tax return (Schedule Q of TP-1) and paid with your annual return
how_and_when_to_pay.deadline: April 30 for payment (June 15 for return filing if self-employed, but interest starts May 1)
how_and_when_to_pay.installments: If you expect to owe more than $1,800 in Quebec taxes including QPP, you must make quarterly installments: March 15, June 15, September 15, December 15
how_and_when_to_pay.practical_tip: Set aside 12.8% of every payment you receive for QPP. Add this to your income tax savings — total self-employment tax burden is roughly 30-40% of net income depending on your income level.

deductions_from_qpp.federal_deduction: You can deduct HALF of your QPP contribution on your federal return (line 22200). This reduces your federal taxable income.
deductions_from_qpp.quebec_tax_credit: Quebec provides a tax credit (not a deduction) for the employee portion of QPP contributions

benefit_of_contributing.retirement_pension: Your contributions build toward a QPP retirement pension, payable as early as age 60 (at reduced rate) or 65 (full rate)
benefit_of_contributing.disability_benefit: QPP also provides disability benefits if you become unable to work
benefit_of_contributing.note: The benefits accumulate based on your contributions and years of contribution — self-employed contributions count fully toward your pension

new_qpp2_enhancement.plain_language: Starting 2024-2026, QPP has an enhanced component (QPP2) that increases the maximum pension. Higher earners pay slightly more but get better benefits.
new_qpp2_enhancement.rate_enhancement: An additional contribution rate applies on earnings between $71,300 and approximately $81,900 (verify current figures)

common_mistakes: Not budgeting for QPP throughout the year and being hit with a $4,000–$8,000 bill in April
Not making quarterly installments when required — penalty is interest charges from the due date
Forgetting to deduct the employer half of QPP on your federal return — you leave money on the table
Confusing QPP (Quebec) with CPP (federal) — Quebec residents with QPP do NOT also pay CPP. They are separate pension plans.

qpp_vs_cpp.important: Quebec has its OWN pension plan (QPP/RRQ). If you work in Quebec, you contribute to QPP, NOT to the federal CPP. Your QPP contributions are administered by Revenu Québec.
qpp_vs_cpp.if_you_worked_in_other_provinces: If you worked in other provinces before Quebec, your CPP contributions transfer and count toward your total pension entitlement
